currency.name: US Dollar (used at all bases)
currency.budgetNote: No shops, no economy. Most visitors come on cruise ships from Argentina or Chile.

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Antarctica on?
mapQuestions.answer: Antarctica IS a continent, the southernmost on Earth, the 5th largest 🌍

mapQuestions.question: Who owns Antarctica?
mapQuestions.answer: NOBODY does. Under the Antarctic Treaty (signed 1959), no country owns Antarctica. It is set aside for peaceful scientific research only, by all countries together.

mapQuestions.question: What language do people speak there?
mapQuestions.answer: Whatever they speak at their home country. There is no native population. Researchers from over 30 countries work at 60+ scientific bases. English is the common working language.

mapQuestions.question: How cold does Antarctica get?
mapQuestions.answer: The coldest temperature ever recorded ANYWHERE on Earth was at the Vostok Station in Antarctica: minus 89.2°C, in 1983. Vostok winters are colder than the surface of Mars at most times.

mapQuestions.question: How much ice does Antarctica have?
mapQuestions.answer: 70% of all the fresh water on Earth is locked up in Antarctic ice. If it all melted, global sea levels would rise about 60 metres.

foods.name: Base canteen meals
foods.description: At every Antarctic research base, food is shipped in by ship or plane months in advance. Meals are hearty, calorie-rich, and surprisingly varied: pasta, soups, roast meats, baked bread, fresh vegetables grown in greenhouses on some modern bases.

foods.name: Fresh-baked bread
foods.description: Every Antarctic base has a baker. Fresh bread is one of the most important morale-boosting foods on the ice. Some bases bake 24/7 in winter.

foods.name: Pemmican (historical)
foods.description: A high-calorie mix of dried meat and fat used by early Antarctic explorers like Scott and Shackleton. It is no longer the main food, but still eaten on long sled trips for energy.

foods.name: Glacier-melt water
foods.description: All drinking water on Antarctic bases comes from melted ice. The water is some of the purest on Earth, having been frozen for thousands of years.

foods.name: Chocolate, lots of it
foods.description: Antarctic researchers and explorers eat 4,000 to 6,000 calories a day to survive the cold. Chocolate, peanut butter, and high-fat snacks are everywhere.

foods.name: Comfort food Sundays
foods.description: Many bases have a tradition of big Sunday roast or themed dinners (Italian night, BBQ night, Christmas in July) to keep spirits up during the long polar winter.

scavengerItems.label: A penguin (Adélie, gentoo, chinstrap, emperor, all live here)
A humpback or minke whale spouting beside the ship
A leopard seal, Weddell seal or crabeater seal on an ice floe
A blue-ice iceberg drifting past your ship
Snow-covered mountains rising straight out of the sea
A research base (a cluster of brightly-painted modular buildings)
A helicopter or Twin Otter plane flying supplies between bases
A glacier "calving" (a huge chunk of ice breaking off into the sea)
A wandering albatross (3+ m wingspan, the biggest of any bird) gliding behind the ship
The aurora australis (Southern Lights) dancing across the polar sky
An historic explorer's hut (Scott's, Shackleton's) preserved frozen in time

geoMatches.description: The long arm of Antarctica that points up toward South America. Most cruises visit here because it has the most accessible ice, penguin colonies and research bases.
The southernmost point on Earth. The Amundsen-Scott South Pole research station sits exactly on the pole. Reached only by ski-plane.
A huge sea on the New Zealand-facing side of Antarctica. Some of the most pristine ecosystem on Earth, with whales, seals and millions of penguins.
The 1,000 km stretch of ocean between South America and Antarctica. Famously rough seas. Crossed by most Antarctic cruise ships in 2 days.
The southernmost active volcano on Earth, on Ross Island. 3,794 m tall, with a permanent lake of bubbling lava in its crater.
A Russian research base in the centre of East Antarctica. Recorded the coldest temperature in Earth's history (minus 89.2°C in 1983). Sits above a giant subglacial lake.

senses.smell: Crisp icy air with no smells at all, salty sea spray near the coast, the surprisingly strong fishy smell of a big penguin colony, diesel from a research base generator…
senses.hear: Wind, always wind, the crack of icebergs splitting, the bark of seals and chatter of penguins, the deep boom of a glacier calving, the silence of the polar night…
senses.taste: Hot chocolate after a cold landing, fresh-baked bread on a ship, the salty zing of Antarctic-caught krill (sometimes served), warm soup against the cold…
senses.feel: Wind chill that can take your breath away, the surprising warmth of a ship's heated lounge, the strange firmness of millennia-old blue ice, the soft snow on the surface of a glacier, the bobbing of a Zodiac landing boat…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How do you get to Antarctica?
stories.body: There are NO commercial flights to Antarctica. The only way to get there as a normal visitor is by ship, almost always leaving from Ushuaia in Argentina (the southernmost city in the world) or Punta Arenas in Chile. Most cruises sail across the famously rough Drake Passage (2 days each way) to the Antarctic Peninsula. A few high-end trips fly across to a small ice runway and then cruise from there. Cruises last between 7 days (peninsula only) and 30+ days (Ross Sea, including South Pole flyovers). All visits to Antarctica are tightly controlled under the Antarctic Treaty: no littering, no touching wildlife, no taking anything away (not even a pebble).

stories.question: Who owns Antarctica?
stories.body: In 1959, with the Cold War in full swing, twelve countries that had researchers in Antarctica (USA, USSR, UK, France, Norway, Argentina, Chile, Japan, New Zealand, Australia, South Africa and Belgium) signed an incredible thing: the Antarctic Treaty. The treaty says Antarctica belongs to no one, can be used only for peaceful scientific research, and no country's flag claim is recognised. Military activity is banned. Nuclear weapons are banned. Mineral exploration is banned. Today over 50 countries have signed the treaty, and 30+ countries run research bases on Antarctica together. It is one of the most successful international agreements in history.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole continent is shared peacefully by dozens of countries for science only. Could humans set up other things in the world like this?

stories.question: How many penguins are there?
stories.body: Antarctica has around 12 million penguins across at least 8 species. The biggest is the emperor penguin (up to 1.3 m tall), which spends the brutal Antarctic winter on the open sea ice while incubating its egg on its feet. The smallest is the Adélie (just 50 cm). Penguins are completely flightless but EXCELLENT swimmers, "flying" through the water on their wings. They eat krill (a small shrimp-like crustacean) and small fish. Some colonies are tens of thousands strong. Walking up to a penguin colony for the first time, with the smell, noise, and parade of waddling birds, is something most people remember for the rest of their lives.

stories.question: Who first reached the South Pole?
stories.body: In 1911 to 1912, two expeditions raced to be the first humans ever to stand at the South Pole. The Norwegian Roald Amundsen, with a small disciplined team of 5 men and dog sleds, reached it on 14 December 1911. The British Robert Falcon Scott, with a bigger team on horse-and-man-hauled sleds, reached the Pole a month later, on 17 January 1912, only to find Amundsen's tent and Norwegian flag already there. Scott and his four companions died on the way back. Their preserved tent and supply depots are still on the ice. Both Amundsen and Scott are remembered with deep respect. The Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station (USA-run) sits exactly on the pole today, and is named after both of them.

stories.question: Why does Antarctica matter for the whole planet?
stories.body: Antarctica holds 70% of all the fresh water on Earth as ice. The Antarctic ice sheets reflect huge amounts of sunlight back into space, helping cool the whole planet. As the world warms, parts of Antarctica are losing ice fast: the Thwaites Glacier in West Antarctica is sometimes called "the Doomsday Glacier" because if it collapses, it could raise global sea levels by half a metre on its own. Scientists from dozens of countries work in Antarctica to study what is happening: drilling ice cores that contain a frozen record of Earth's atmosphere going back 800,000 years, tracking how ice melts, measuring climate change in the most direct way possible. Antarctica is the planet's early-warning system.

convoQuestions.question: What was the first thing that surprised you about Antarctica?
Did you see a penguin colony up close? What did it smell like?
Ice, mountains, wildlife, weather, which has felt the most "Antarctica"?
How does silence feel here compared to anywhere else?
If you could spend ONE week working at an Antarctic research base, what would you want to research?
Antarctica is shared peacefully by 50+ countries for science. What human problems could we solve if we agreed to share other things like that?

wordSearch.hint: Antarctic places, animals and things to spot.

animals.name: Emperor penguin
animals.description: The biggest penguin (up to 1.3 m tall). Breeds in the dead of Antarctic winter on the open sea ice. Males balance the egg on their feet for two months.

animals.name: Adélie penguin
animals.description: A small black-and-white penguin (50 cm), often seen waddling in big colonies on rocky coasts.

animals.name: Humpback whale
animals.description: The most-seen whale on Antarctic cruises. Feeds on krill, breaches near boats, sometimes "spy hops" to look at you.

animals.name: Leopard seal
animals.description: A long, powerful, spotted seal. The main predator of Adélie penguins. Will sometimes lift its head out of the water and stare you down from an ice floe.

animals.name: Crabeater seal
animals.description: Despite the name, eats krill (not crabs). The most common seal on Earth, with around 15 million in Antarctic waters.

animals.name: Wandering albatross
animals.description: Has the largest wingspan of any living bird (up to 3.5 m). Glides for thousands of kilometres without flapping.

animals.name: Orca (killer whale)
animals.description: Big black-and-white dolphins that hunt seals and penguins in coordinated pods. Several Antarctic orca pods specialise in different hunting strategies.
